segments.s358-g49: The clause is compact: بستدی is an archaic past form (3rd‑person singular) of بستن ‘to bind/close’ and serves as the main verb. نیز follows the verb and functions adverbially ‘also’; هر سال is the time adverbial ‘every year’ (هر modifies سال). باژ is a noun placed after the temporal phrase and here is the thing associated with the action (object or what is given/required). Word order is verb (past) + adverbial + temporal phrase + noun, a common flexible order in classical Persian.
segments.s358-g50: چرا here is an archaic verbal/nominal element related to grazing/feeding (not the modern interrogative); داد is the verb ‘gave’ (or a light verb marking transfer) and appears with باید ‘must/ought’ which expresses necessity — باید follows داد in the clause but scopes over the action (‘must give’). به introduces the beneficiary ‘to/for,’ هامال is the beneficiary ‘porter/burden‑bearer,’ and the final باژ names the thing to be given (a ‘baž’, translated here as ‘cry’ or a small due). The sequence shows classical flexibility: object/related noun can appear before the modal auxiliary باید and again at the end.
segments.s358-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The construction here is slightly inverted: 'گشتاسپ را' (Gushtasp as the marked recipient) appears before the verb داد 'gave', and the subject لهراسپ and the thing given تخت follow the verb — a common poetic OVS/OV order. را is the definite object marker marking گشتاسپ as the indirect object/recipient.
segments.s358-g2: فرود آمد is a compound past tense meaning 'descended' / 'came down'. از تخت specifies source 'from the throne'. The second clause 'و بربست رخت' uses the transitive verb بربستن in past form (بربست) meaning 'bound up/packed (his) belongings'; رخت here means 'baggage/possessions'.
segments.s358-g3: گزین شد is a passive/impersonal perfect meaning 'was chosen/appointed'. به بلخ gives the destination or assignment 'to/for Balkh'. بران نوبهار is a temporal phrase 'that new spring' (بران = 'upon/at that', نوبهار = 'new spring'), specifying when the appointment happened.
segments.s358-g4: که introduces a subordinate clause (here 'that/which' or 'for that'). یزدان‌پرستان is the noun phrase 'God‑worshippers' (subject). بدان روزگار is an adverbial phrase 'in that time'; orthographically split here into ب + دان, with ب functioning like the preposition 'to/in' and دان representing the demonstrative 'that' — an archaic form (see senses).
segments.s358-g5: مران is a proper-name group (a people/place) here and functions as the subject. جای را is the definite object 'the place' (را marks it) placed before the verb. داشتندی is an archaic/persianized past plural form meaning 'they had/possessed' (classical poetic ending -ندی). چنان ('such/so') links to the comparative clause that follows.
segments.s358-g6: که introduces the comparison 'as/like'. مکه را is 'Mecca' with را marking it as the object; تازیان is the subject 'the Arabs' and این زمان is the temporal adverbial 'in this time/now'. The item مر is an archaic or uncertain particle in this line and its exact sense is unclear in isolation (hence left as unspecified).
segments.s358-g7: بدان is the archaic prepositional phrase 'to that' (here written as ب + دان), equivalent to Modern Persian 'به آن'. شد with a motion sense yields 'went/arrived' (an idiomatic use in older Persian: 'به خانه شد' = 'he went into the house'). شاه یزدان‌پرست is the noun phrase 'the God‑worshipping king' identifying the actor.
segments.s358-g8: فرود آمد is 'he descended / came down' (past), from جایگاه 'from his place/seat' (از marks the source). نشست is the simple past 'sat down' or 'took his seat', completing the sequence of actions. The two verbs describe consecutive motions: descending and then sitting.
segments.s358-g9: ببست (past verb) is a simple past/punctual verb meaning “shut” and comes first here (verb–object order is common in poetry). آن در آفرین خانه را is the object phrase: آن = “that,” در = “door,” آفرین خانه = a genitive-like compound “house of praise/worship,” and را is the definite/object marker attaching to the whole noun phrase. The clause therefore literally reads “he shut that door of the praise-house.”
segments.s358-g10: نماند is a past-stative verb “was left / remained (not)” used here negatively (no one remained). اندرو = اندر + او, “in it / inside,” places the remainder inside the house. خویش (written as خوی + ش) = “his own / kin” with the enclitic ش marking possession. و = “and,” بیگانه = “stranger,” and the trailing را is topical/definite marking the phrase. Word order places the locative (اندرو) before the nouns being negated: “Inside it (اندرو) neither his own nor the stranger remained.”
segments.s358-g11: بپوشید is the past tense of the verb پوشیدن “to put on / wear.” جامه پرستش is a noun phrase where جامه = “garment/robe” and پرستش = “worship,” so together “the garment of worship.” پلاس is an appositive or clarifying noun “a coarse cloak / blanket.” The sequence says he donned a worship-robe, i.e. he put on the coarse cloak used for worship.
segments.s358-g12: خرد را is the object-marked noun “wisdom / the mind,” with را marking it as definite/object. چنان کرد is a light verb construction: چنان = “so/thus,” کرد = “made/did,” together “made it so.” باید سپاس is a modal + noun: باید = “must / it is necessary,” سپاس = “thanks / praise.” Grammatically the clause says he “made his wisdom/heart such that thanksgiving is required” (i.e. he put himself into a state that demands thanks).
segments.s358-g13: بیفگند is a past-tense verb from افکندن “to throw / cast,” here used idiomatically “to let fall / cast down.” یاره فرو هشت موی is a poetic description: فرو = “down,” موی = “hair,” and هشت (eight) qualifies the hair as falling in tresses; یاره is a modifier (poetic/archaic) here appearing with the hair image. The whole phrase describes letting the locks fall down.
segments.s358-g14: سوی روشن = “toward the bright/illuminated (one)” where سوی is the preposition “toward(s)” and روشن is the adjective “bright/clear.” دادگر = “the Just / righteous (ruler or judge)” functions as the target of the turn of face. کرد + روی is a light-verb construction: کرد = “made/did,” روی = “face,” so کرد روی = “turned (his) face.” Together: “he turned his face toward the shining Just One.”
segments.s358-g15: همی بود is an iterative/progressive past marker in poetry: همی + بود conveys “he used to be / he was continually.” سی سال = “thirty years.” پیشش here means “before him / in his presence.” به پای = a locative phrase “at the feet (of).” The clause therefore says that for thirty years he continually remained at (someone’s) feet, i.e. in subservience.
segments.s358-g16: برینسان (from برینس) = “princes / prince” acts as the object or target of پرستید = past “worshipped / adored.” The final بیت-level statement بایَد خدای places a moral judgment: باید = “ought / must,” and خدای = “God,” implying that instead of worshipping princes he ought to worship God (or that praise belongs to God). Grammatically the sequence contrasts past worship of princes with the obligation toward God.
segments.s358-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. گیتی بران is an inverted prepositional phrase meaning 'over/ upon that world' (گیتی = 'world', بران = 'on/over that'), with the postposition-like element بران following the noun. شاه نو راست شد is the core verb phrase: شاه نو 'a new king' + راست شد, where راست (here 'right/established') + شد is an inchoative/past 'became/was set' construction.
segments.s358-g42: فریدون is the subject (proper name). دیگر is an adverb meaning 'again/another (time)' and همی is the archaic progressive/preservative particle marking continued or habitual aspect. خواست is the verbal stem 'desire/wish' and شد is the auxiliary 'became' producing a periphrastic past/inchoative sense 'became desirous / wished again.' Word order places modifiers before the verb cluster.
segments.s358-g43: گزیدش is the past verb گزید ('chose') with the object enclitic -ش 'him' attached, so 'chose him.' بدادند is the past plural 'they gave' (here functioning as a light/auxiliary or emphatic accompaniment to گزید), and شاهان همه ('all the kings') is the agent; the subject is placed after the verb cluster (poetic inversion) for emphasis.
segments.s358-g44: نشستن is a deverbal or gerundial use meaning 'the settling/being at rest' (here of feelings). دل نیک‌خواهان is a genitive: 'the heart(s) of the well‑wishers.' همه quantifies 'all.' The phrase thus reads 'the settling of the hearts of all well‑wishers' = 'the hearts of all well‑wishers were at rest.'
segments.s358-g45: مگر introduces an exception ('except'). شاه ارجاسپ توران is apposition: 'the king, Arjasp of Turan.' خدای here is an epithet/element following the name; in classical usage it can function as a title or exclamatory particle (the exact nuance is poetic/archaic). The phrase as a whole names the sole exception.
segments.s358-g46: که is the relative 'who/whom' linking to the preceding Arjasp. دیوان here (contextually 'the divs' = demons) acts as the subject — this semantic sense is not listed in the provided sense options, so it is marked -1. بدندی is an archaic past/intransitive form (دَندیدن) meaning 'they bared their teeth / glared / showed hostility.' به پیشش به پای is a prepositional cluster 'before him, at his feet'—به پیشش 'in his presence/before him' and به پای 'at (his) feet.'
segments.s358-g47: گزیتش is a proper name (Gazit) with the enclitic -ش (him) attached in the surface token; here it functions as the actor/name. نپذرفت is the negated past 'did not accept/refuse(d).' و is the coordinating 'and.' نشنید پند is 'did not hear/heed advice' — نشنید is past negative of 'hear' and پند is its object.
segments.s358-g48: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' پند نشنید repeats the protasis 'if he did not hear/heed counsel' (پند = counsel/advice; نشنید = 'did not hear'). زو is the poetic contraction of ز + او meaning 'from him/it' or 'because of it.' دید is the past 'saw' (here 'found/experienced'), and بند is 'bond/fetter' (i.e. captivity). The clause thus says 'if he did not heed counsel, from that he saw/experienced bondage.'
segments.s358-g33: پس is a discourse/temporal particle 'then'. آن دختر نامور قیصرا is a noun phrase in the order determiner (آن) + noun (دختر) + adjective (نامور) + title/name in the genitive-like apposition (قیصرا) — literally 'then that famed daughter of the Caesar'.
segments.s358-g34: The clause begins with the relative/connector که 'who/whom'. ناهید is the proper name of the goddess (here agent). بد نام is a collocation meaning 'badly named / ill-reputed' (بد modifies نام understood as 'reputation'). آن دخترا ('those maidens' / 'that maiden(s)') is the object/affected group; Persian often omits an explicit verb, so this relative clause states that Nahid caused a bad reputation for those maidens.
segments.s358-g35: کتایونش is the proper name Katayun plus the enclitic -ش serving as object 'her' (i.e. 'called her Katayun'). خواندی is a past tense verb 'he called/named' (here sense 'to call/name'). گرانمایه 'precious/esteemed' is an epithet; شاه 'the king' appears after the verb as the agent (typical older Persian word order: object — verb — subject).
segments.s358-g36: دو فرزندش is a definite noun phrase: numeral 'two' + noun 'children' + enclitic ش 'his' ('his two children'). آمد is the past tense 'came'. چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as', and تابنده ماه is a simile 'the shining moon' (تابنده modifies ماه). Together: 'his two children came like the shining moon.'
segments.s358-g37: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'one / a certain'. It is followed by epithets 'نامور فرخ' (renowned, fortunate) which precede the proper name اسفندیار; this is a typical epic appositional description 'a certain famed, fortunate Esfandiyar.'
segments.s358-g38: This phrase strings together titles/attributes without an overt relative pronoun. شه is a royal epithet/prefix, کارزاری 'martial / of war' qualifies that status, نبرده is the negative perfect/past participle 'not-taken / not-captured' (ne + برده), and سوار 'rider' names the warrior type. Together they describe him: a royal/martial rider who had not been taken (i.e. undefeated or uncaptured).
segments.s358-g39: پشوتن دگر = 'the other, Peshotan' (دگر 'other/another' after the name is common in epic diction). گرد شمشیر زن is a compound-like descriptive unit: گرد here contributes a verbal/adverbial sense 'going about/brandishing' combined with شمشیر 'sword' + زن (from زدن 'to strike') forming 'sword-striker' i.e. 'sword-wielding warrior.' The sequence describes Peshotan as the other, a sword-wielding fighter.
segments.s358-g40: This short phrase gives further epithets: شه as a royal/title element, نامبردار 'renowned' (an honorific adjective), and لشکرشکن 'army-breaker' (a compound noun meaning one who shatters armies). Together they function as appositional titles applied to Peshotan.
segments.s358-g25: بدان is a classical/poetic adverbial compound (ب‑دان) meaning 'thereupon / to that effect'; داد is the past verb 'gave' and appears before its object phrase (poetic inversion). ما را is the indirect/object phrase 'to us' (ما + object marker را). کلاه بزرگ is the direct object 'a great hat/cap'.
segments.s358-g26: که here introduces purpose/result ('so that'); بیرون is the adverb 'out/away' and کنیم is the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/jussive of کردن ('we may/might do/drive out'). از marks origin ('from/out of'), رم means 'flight/stampede' and qualifies میش ('ram/ewes'), while گرگ is the agent/object being driven out ('the wolf').
segments.s358-g27: سوی راه یزدان is an adverbial phrase 'toward the way/path of God'. بیازیم is an archaic/poetic first‑person‑plural form (hortative/subjunctive) meaning 'we let loose, we break forth' (here 'we strike/play'); چنگ is the object 'harp' placed after the verb by poetic order.
segments.s358-g28: بر here functions as 'toward / with regard to'; آزاده گیتی is a genitival phrase 'the world's free/noble (people)' where آزاده = 'noble/free' and گیتی = 'world'. نداریم is the present negative 'we do not have / we are not', and تنگ ('narrow') is a predicative adjective meaning 'stingy / close‑fisted' (so 'we are not stingy toward the noble of the world').
segments.s358-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آیین شاهان بجای آوریم is a verbal phrase 'we bring the kings' rites back into place / we restore the rites of kings' (بجای آوردن = 'to restore/put in place'). آوریم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive used in a general/future sense.
segments.s358-g30: بدان here functions as 'to that / that thing' (classical usage); را is the definite‑object marker attached to that demonstrative. به دین خدای آوریم = 'we bring (it) into the religion of God' where به = 'into/to', دین خدای = 'God's religion', and آوریم = 'we bring/put'.
segments.s358-g31: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone'; داد گسترد is a verbal phrase 'spread/gave widely' (گسترد = 'extended, spread' modifying/strengthening داد). کز is a relative/prepositional 'from which / from whom' linking to the next clause; داد اوی = 'his giving' (اوی as possessive 'his').
segments.s358-g32: ابا is the verb 'to be reluctant / refrain' (here: 'refrained'); the following noun phrase names the thing refrained from: گرگ (the wolf) + میش آب‑خوردی به جوی (an attributive construction: 'the ewe that was drinking water at the stream'). خوردی functions as a verbal/participial element 'drinking/drank (water)'.
segments.s358-g17: نیایش + کردن: a nominal verb construction where the noun نیایش ('prayer') combines with the light verb کرد to mean 'to pray' or 'to worship.' همی + کرد is the archaic/historic-habitual form (narrative imperfect) meaning 'kept doing' or 'was continually doing.' خورشید is the direct object and را is the definite/object marker placed after it.
segments.s358-g18: چنان بوده is a past/perfect-stative phrase 'thus/so it was.' بد راه is an adjective + noun sequence 'bad way/course.' جمشید را places the name with the object/topic marker را (classical inversion), so the whole phrase reads 'such had been the bad way of Jamshid' (i.e. 'Jamshid's bad course').
segments.s358-g19: چو here is the conjunction 'when.' گشتاسپ is the subject. بر + شد is a verbal compound meaning 'mounted' or 'rose up onto' (بر = 'upon/on' + شد = 'became/was/rose'). به تخت پدر is a prepositional phrase 'onto/ to the father's throne.'
segments.s358-g20: که introduces the following explanatory/relative clause. هم here means 'also' or 'too' (in addition). فر او داشت: فر ('divine royal glory') is the direct object and داشت is the past-tense possession verb 'had.' و links the next noun phrase; بخت پدر ('the father's bāxt') is coordinated and understood as also possessed ('and had his father's fortune').
segments.s358-g21: به سر بر نهاد is an inverted adverbial + verb order meaning 'placed on (his) head' (به سر = 'on the head'; بر نهاد = past 'put on/placed upon'). آن پدر داده تاج is a noun phrase where پدر داده ('father-given') modifies تاج ('crown') — classical order places the giver before the past-participle modifier.
segments.s358-g22: که introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause. زیبنده is a predicate adjective 'befitting'; باشد is the optative/subjunctive form 'may be / be (so)' expressing purpose or desirability. بر here has a benefactive/for sense ('for' or 'upon' someone); آزاده modifies the implied wearer as 'noble'/'free-hearted', and تاج repeats the idea 'the crown' — i.e. 'that the crown might be fitting for a noble.'
segments.s358-g23: This is a speech tag: منم is the emphatic 'I am' or 'it is I.' گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (classical narrative places the quoted formula in this order). یزدان پرستنده is a compound adjective 'God-worshipping' modifying شاه ('the king'). The whole reads: '"It is I," said the God-worshipping king.'
segments.s358-g24: This is a simple verb clause with inverted/old-fashioned order: مرا = 'to me' (من + را) as the indirect object, ایزد پاک is the subject 'the pure God,' داد is the past verb 'gave,' and این کلاه is the direct object 'this cap/hat.' Literal order: 'To me the pure God gave this hat.'

sentences.s358-s25: He bound him too; each year he must give the porter a baž (a cry/fee).
sentences.s358-s1: When Lohrasp gave the throne to Gushtasp, he descended from the throne and packed up his belongings.
sentences.s358-s2: That spring he was appointed to Balkh, for the God‑worshippers in those times...
sentences.s358-s3: The Maran possessed that place, such as the Arabs possess Mecca in our time.
sentences.s358-s4: To that house went the pious king; he descended from his place and sat down.
sentences.s358-s5: He shut that door of the house of praise; neither his own (kinsmen) nor a stranger remained inside it.
sentences.s358-s6: He put on the garment of worship — a coarse cloak — and made his mind such that he must give thanks.
sentences.s358-s7: He let his hair fall in eight tresses; then he turned his face toward the shining Just One.
sentences.s358-s8: For thirty years he remained at his feet before him; for thirty years he had been at his feet, worshipping princes — he ought to worship God.
sentences.s358-s21: When a new king was firmly set over that world, Fereydun again became desirous.
sentences.s358-s22: All the kings chose him, and the hearts of all well‑wishers were at rest.
sentences.s358-s23: Except for Arjasp, the Turanian king, before whom the divs (demons) bared their teeth at his feet.
sentences.s358-s24: Gazit refused him and did not heed counsel; if he did not hear counsel, thereby he found himself in bonds.
sentences.s358-s17: Then that famed daughter of the Caesar — whom the goddess Nahid had given a bad reputation among the maidens.
sentences.s358-s18: The king named her Katayun, the precious; his two children came like the shining moon.
sentences.s358-s19: One — the famed, fortunate Esfandiyar: a warlike (royal) rider who had not been taken.
sentences.s358-s20: The other was Peshotan — a sword-wielding warrior, a royal renowned breaker of armies.
sentences.s358-s13: Thereupon he gave us a great cap, so that we might drive the wolf out of the ewes' stampede.
sentences.s358-s14: Toward God's path we shall strike/let loose the harp; toward the noble of the world we are not stingy.
sentences.s358-s15: When we restore the rites of kings, we will bring that into God's religion.
sentences.s358-s16: A certain man gave generously; from his giving the wolf refrained from attacking the ewe that drank at the stream.
sentences.s358-s9: He kept worshipping the sun; such was Jamshid's evil way.
sentences.s358-s10: When Gushtasp mounted his father's throne, he had both (his) farr and his father's fortune.
sentences.s358-s11: He placed on his head that crown given by his father, so that the crown would be fitting for a noble.
sentences.s358-s12: "It is I," said the God-worshipping king; "the pure God gave me this cap."
